It’s a quantitative financial analytics platform designed for stock and crypto traders who want to move past guesswork and gut-feeling decisions.

Walked into this panel on digital assets policy not knowing much about the space — walked out with a much clearer picture of where crypto regulation stands and why it matters far beyond crypto.

Big takeaways: the GENIUS Act has stablecoins starting to plug into real banking infrastructure, and the CLARITY Act is the industry’s next big push — basically trying to give builders clear rules instead of years of regulation-by-enforcement.

What surprised me most was how much overlap there is with AI policy. Several panelists came from crypto policy and said the AI world is about to face the exact same fights: get ahead of how regulators define your technology (crypto’s definitions came from the tax code, of all places, and they stuck for good), build coalitions early, keep it bipartisan, and educate before you legislate.

Also interesting: everyone seems to think prediction markets and “agentic” payments (AI agents transacting on their own) are the next frontier once CLARITY gets sorted.
Good reminder that a lot of today’s tech policy fights are really dress rehearsals for tomorrow’s.

Thank you - Stacey Rolland, Jessica Martinez, Ellie Small, and Gilberto Nazario

The Biggest Financial Mistake I See Homeowners Make Has Nothing to Do With Their Mortgage

When people think about building wealth through real estate, they usually focus on one thing.

Buying the right house.

In my experience, that's rarely what separates homeowners who build significant wealth from those who don't.

The biggest difference is how long they own it.

Historically, home values have appreciated over time. The people who benefited the most weren't the ones who perfectly timed the market. They were the ones who stayed invested long enough for time to do the heavy lifting.

I've seen buyers hesitate because they were waiting for lower interest rates.
I've seen sellers delay because they thought prices might climb another 5%.
Sometimes they're right.

Many times they aren't.

The market will always give you reasons to wait.

Life usually won't.

Job opportunities change.

Families grow.

Kids start school.

Retirement gets closer.

Those life events matter more than perfectly timing a market that no one can consistently predict.

That doesn't mean you should buy a home you can't comfortably afford.
It means your long-term financial decisions should be driven by your goals, not headlines.

The buyers who tend to do the best aren't the ones trying to outsmart the market.

They're the ones who make smart decisions and give those decisions time to work.

If you're thinking about buying or selling in the Treasure Valley, start with a plan instead of a prediction. Markets change. A good strategy adapts with them.

If you found this article valuable, I'd love to connect with you here on LinkedIn. I share practical market insights each week to help buyers and sellers make informed real estate decisions.

《Kimi K3:a stress test for the entire AI valuation premium》

On July 16, Moonshot AI unveiled K3: a 2.8‑trillion‑parameter Mixture‑of‑Experts architecture with a 1M token context window, native multimodal understanding, and a promise to open‑source full weights by July 27. It will be the largest open‑weight model ever – and it’s already reshaping markets.

The numbers that turned heads:

· Arena Frontend Code ranking: #1 with 1,679 Elo – a 17‑place jump from its predecessor.
· Head‑to‑head blind wins: 76% vs. Claude Fable 5 (which scored 63%) and GPT‑5.6 Sol (58%).
· Top in 6 out of 7 frontend sub‑categories.

Elon Musk called it “impressive” on X.

But the real story is the political and financial blowback.

OpenAI’s Head of Strategic Futures, Dean W. Ball, published “Some observations on Kimi.” He conceded that K3 is a “very good model” whose agentic coding skills approach early‑2026’s best public models. That’s a significant admission from a lab that has long accused Chinese players of free‑riding. He then pivoted: open‑weight models are “inherently decelerationist” because they undermine monetisation of frontier AI. His proposed US response? Not an outright ban, but “regulatory fog” – soft warnings about backdoors and security to scare regulated enterprises away from Chinese open source. Whether that’s policy genius or competitive self‑interest is for you to judge.

Demand went supernova. Within 48 hours, GPU clusters were overwhelmed. On July 19, Moonshot paused new consumer subscriptions – a rare sign of a product people actually want to pay for.

Now for the part that’s moving markets:

Pre‑IPO valuations for Anthropic and OpenAI have taken a combined **~1.557T implied), OpenAI fell 5.6% (to ~$1.238T). Prediction markets now peg the probability that Anthropic reaches a $1.5T valuation by year‑end at just 67% – down 11 points.

Why such a violent reaction? Because K3 dismantles two core narratives:

1. “The West has an insurmountable lead.” Berkeley’s top AI lab now puts the gap between US and Chinese frontier models at “roughly two to three months.”
2. “Compute is the only moat.” K3’s API pricing is ~30% of Claude Fable 5’s, challenging the idea that billions in GPU spending guarantees superior returns.

Nvidia caught a stray too – single‑day market cap loss of ~$111B as investors reassessed the scaling thesis.

And in a twist, Moonshot itself is reportedly eyeing a Hong Kong IPO within six months at a valuation target north of $30B.

So what does this mean?

For two years, the narrative was that Chinese labs were “catching up.” K3 suggests they’ve arrived. If Moonshot open‑sources 2.8T parameters on July 27, the closed‑source moat isn’t just narrower – it’s porous. The IPO market is waking up to a world where open‑weight models compete head‑on with premium offerings, and the old pricing power may not hold.

The race isn’t over. But the valuation math just got rewritten.… more

Prediction Markets Are Minting a New Type of Insider Trader
Sites like Polymarket were built to price uncertainty. New data shows they might be rewarding privileged knowledge instead.
By 
Denitsa Tsekova
Leonardo Nicoletti
July 21, 2026
In early February, Caden Booth, a 21-year-old TikToker, flew from Cincinnati to San Francisco and headed for the stadium where Super Bowl LX was taking place. He waited 12 hours, until he heard rehearsals for the national anthem begin. Stopwatch in hand, he timed how long each version took. Then he used that data to bet more than $50,000 on Polymarket that the anthem’s length would be under 117 seconds on game day — and scored a massive win when it turned out to be 104.
After Booth turned the episode into a viral video, accusations of “insider trading” started piling up. He didn’t appear to have broken any rules: He’d simply researched when rehearsals typically take place and listened from a public sidewalk. “People were saying, ‘Is this illegal? How is this allowed?’’’ Booth recalls. “A lot of people took it as, ‘Does this hurt the integrity of prediction markets, because this kid can just easily go time the anthem?’”
Like other types of markets, prediction markets reward traders who get information first. So it stands to reason that, as sites such as Kalshi and Polymarket become more widespread and high-stakes, they’re being scrutinized for the possibility that some people have unequal, even unfair, access to that data. Some saw Booth’s wagers, however dubiously, as crossing the line between enterprising and exploitative approaches to getting information.… more

France's gambling regulator ordered ISPs to block Polymarket, classifying the prediction market as illegal gambling rather than a financial trading venue. That reclassification is the whole fight, and it's a fight prediction markets keep losing.

Here's the structural problem. A prediction market and a sportsbook look identical from the outside — real-time odds, binary outcomes, money on the line. The distinction regulators are supposed to draw is functional: is this a wager against the house, or a market where price discovery reflects genuine information about a future event?

France didn't draw that line. It looked at the interface — the dynamically displaying homepage odds — and called the display itself the promotion of unauthorized gambling. When the regulatory test collapses into "it looks like a betting site," every prediction market loses, because they all look like betting sites.

And the enforcement mechanism tells you how weak the position is. A ban a VPN defeats isn't regulation, it's a press release. 205,000 French users were already routing around the November 2024 financial-transaction ban. Ordering ISPs to block the URL just moves the whole population one browser extension to the left.

The uncomfortable part for the industry: France isn't wrong that Polymarket lacks stake limits and self-exclusion tools. The functional-versus-cosmetic distinction only protects prediction markets if the markets actually behave like information venues and not casinos. Thirty-plus jurisdictions have now voted with their firewalls.

Is there a coherent test that separates an information market from a wager without collapsing into "does it show odds on a screen"? Nobody regulating this has articulated one yet.

The CFTC just preempted Michigan by resurrecting prediction-market trades the state moved to ban. A Michigan court had ordered KalshiEX to cancel trades its residents already executed; the CFTC stayed Kalshi's rule change and directed the exchange to honor them.

Strip away the prediction-market noise and the fight is older: whether one state can reach into a national derivatives market and void settled contracts after the fact.

It can't, and the reason is structural. A federally regulated exchange owes every participant impartial access and settled trades that stay settled. Let fifty states rewind executed positions whenever a local court objects, and no counterparty can price risk on that exchange. The market stops being national the moment it stops being uniform. Law has to accommodate that structure, not the other way around.

The CFTC picked its ground well. It didn't defend whether event contracts belong on an exchange, a fair fight for Congress. It defended finality, where it holds the stronger hand.

Whether prediction markets belong on a derivatives exchange is worth arguing. A state court tearing up cleared trades is not the way to argue it.… more

One thing I have noticed over the years is that uncertainty can make people pause for a very long time.

In property, that often comes up when the market feels noisy. Interest rates are moving, borrowing capacity changes, headlines become louder and people start waiting for the “right” moment to feel obvious.

The difficult part is that the perfect moment rarely announces itself.

There will always be a reason to hesitate. There will always be a headline, a prediction, a market shift or a conversation that makes people second-guess what they were planning to do.

That is where having a clear strategy matters.

For buyers and investors, the decision should always come back to their own numbers, their own goals and the quality of the property they are considering. Can they afford to hold it comfortably? Does the location have good long-term appeal? Is there rental demand? Does the property suit the plan they have in place?

Those questions are much more helpful than trying to react to every short-term movement in the market.

I have seen people make very good property decisions in all different market conditions, and the common thread is usually clarity. They understand their position, they get the right advice and they make decisions based on what works for them rather than waiting for every part of the market to feel certain.

That also takes a bit of discipline.

When the market feels uncertain, some people panic. Some people freeze. Others take the time to review their position properly, ask better questions and move forward when the numbers and the opportunity make sense.

Property is a long-term decision for most people. The right move should be considered carefully, but waiting indefinitely can become its own decision too.

There is a difference between being cautious and being stuck.

For anyone looking at their next property move, whether that is buying, selling, upgrading or investing, my advice is to get clear on your own position first. Speak with your broker, understand your budget, look carefully at the property itself and get advice from people who know the local market.

The more clarity you have, the easier it becomes to make decisions calmly, even when the market around you feels uncertain.… more

Australia is standing in the middle of its vehicle transition with an uncomfortable truth becoming harder to ignore. The emperor is naked. Not partially dressed. Not missing a sleeve. Completely exposed. The real question is how a country with genuine leverage ended up naked.

The New Vehicle Efficiency Standard arrived in 2024 with the confidence of a nation stepping into modernity. The announcement sounded like momentum. It sounded like progress. It sounded like leadership finally catching up. But anyone who understands industrial strategy could see the timing was off. Other Western markets had already shaped their transitions years earlier. Europe had negotiated industrial return before tightening standards. The US had tied incentives to domestic manufacturing. China had already built the scale Australia now rewards.

Australia arrived late, but acted like it was early — as if the leverage was still there. We stepped forward as if we were shaping the global transition rather than joining it after the hard work had already been done elsewhere. And the nations that invested in us — the ones that built capability here, the ones that anchored supply chains here, the ones that treated Australia as a stable, predictable market — watched us discard them without a conversation. Investors saw a country willing to abandon incumbents without securing commitments from new entrants. They saw a nation rushing to validate others while forgetting to protect itself.

The emperor didn’t lose the robe through theft. It was handed away through timing, sequencing and a belief that announcing progress was the same as securing value. And the world noticed. 

That’s how the emperor ended up naked.

Everyone had Argentina winning the World Cup final.

Prediction models, betting markets, pundits, fans — practically all of them.
Spain won 1-0 in extra time.

I build fraud detection and forecasting models for a living, and this is exactly the failure mode I think about every day. Not "the model was wrong" — models are wrong all the time. What matters is whether the model knew it could be wrong.
In ML, this is called calibration.

A model can say "Argentina has a 75% chance to win" — and that's not the same as "Argentina will win." A well-calibrated model that says 75% should be wrong 1 out of every 4 times it says that. If it's never wrong, it's not confident, it's overconfident — and overconfidence is more dangerous than inaccuracy, because it convinces people to stop questioning it.

A few reasons predictions like this break down, and why I design around them:
→ Models are trained on history, and history has a distribution. Upsets happen exactly when reality steps outside that distribution — what some call a tail event. No amount of past data fully protects you from what hasn't happened yet.
→ When every source — pundits, markets, models — converges on the same favorite, that's not independent confirmation. It's usually the same bias (recent form, star power, past titles) baked into everyone's inputs. Agreement isn't the same as being right.
→ The best systems don't hand you a single confident number. They hand you a range, a probability, a "here's my confidence and here's why" — and they leave room for a human to make the final call.

That last point is the one I apply directly in my own work. Whether it's flagging a fraudulent transaction or letting an AI copilot answer a compliance question, I never let the model's confidence be the final word. A probability score, not a verdict. A source citation, not a black box. A human checkpoint before anything high-stakes happens.

Spain didn't win because the models were bad. They won because the model builders forgot that "most likely" was never a guarantee.

3. Kimi K3 Narrows The Intelligence Gap, Not The Compute Bill
By: Frank Downing

On the heels of the deluge of model launches over the past several months, China-based Moonshot AI released Kimi K3, a 2.8-trillion-parameter mixture-of-experts model. Moonshot plans to publish the model weights by July 27, at which point K3 would become the largest open-weight model released to date. Until then, K3 will be available only through Moonshot’s applications and Application Programming Interface (API). K3’s performance seems impressive, as shown below. 

The model scored 57 on the Artificial Analysis Intelligence Index,11 just below Claude Fable 5 at 60 and GPT-5.6 Sol at 59, and ahead of Claude Opus 4.8 and GPT 5.6. Though K3 has been available for just hours, an open-weight model which might outperform Opus, within striking distance of Fable and GPT-5.6 Sol, could be game-changing competitively, though real-world reliability and the sustainability of early benchmark results will be telling.

Perhaps more interesting is K3’s cost profile. According to Artificial Analysis, K3 costs ~$0.95 per task on average, compared with $1.04 for GPT-5.6 Sol, $1.80 for Claude Opus 4.8, and $2.75 for Claude Fable 5. While competitive with proprietary frontier models, K3 is not selling at the steep discount of earlier Chinese releases. Moonshot charges $15 per million output tokens for K3, nearly 4x the $4 for Kimi K2.6, suggesting that scaling its model has raised inference costs. Even so, K3 is less expensive than several of its frontier model peers.

The equity market’s reaction was like the DeepSeek shock in early 2025. On Friday, Hong Kong-listed Z.ai—a competitive open-weight frontier developer—fell 28.5% and MiniMax, 15.6%. Amplifying a decline already underway, semiconductor stocks continued to sell off. Despite a ~36% equity interest in Moonshot as disclosed in its fiscal 2024 annual report, Alibaba’s stock closed down as well.

That said, open weights do not mean free intelligence. Moonshot recommends deploying K3 on supernodes with at least 64 accelerators, infrastructure that is scarce and concentrated largely among hyperscalers and specialized AI clouds. Moreover, the agentic applications built on top of an open model are not free. If model intelligence does commoditize, more value should migrate toward the infrastructure that serves models and the agent layer that turns them into useful products.

Today, OpenAI and Anthropic lead in widely used consumer and developer agents through ChatGPT, Codex, and Claude, while platforms like Palantir AIP provide model-agnostic enterprise orchestration, governance, and deployment. In our view, the open-weight disruption narrative will have teeth only if models like K3 create agentic products that challenge the distribution advantages of competitors.

Investor Gavin Baker argued the release could be broadly positive for most companies while squeezing the roughly 90% inference margins currently enjoyed by frontier model builders.

Critics of the alarm pushed back, noting that Kimi K3 consumes far more tokens per task than comparable U.S. models due to its verbose, reasoning-heavy design. Baker estimated the true cost to run the model is 50% to 70% higher than similar U.S. alternatives, making low headline pricing a somewhat misleading comparison.

Prediction market traders on Polymarket remained relatively unfazed, with the contract on the best AI model at year-end still assigning Anthropic more than 67% probability and Moonshot sitting in the low single digits. The market's muted reaction suggests investors see Kimi K3 as competitive pressure rather than evidence of a fundamental shift in AI leadership.

ICYMI: This recently released paper by Kalshi notifies us that the prediction market (aka betting?) is now aiming to include the outcome of Phase 3 clinical trials.

There's a lot to unpack in teh paper but I'd like to highlight one particu;ar area, the fact that our conflict-of-interest rules have not kept pace.

Kalshi has launched contracts allowing people to take a direct financial position on whether a named Phase 3 trial will meet its primary endpoint. It prohibits trading by anyone holding material nonpublic information, which is clearly necessary. However, the more difficult question is how that prohibition will work across the fragmented clinical-trial ecosystem.

FDA’s financial-disclosure requirements under 21 CFR Part 54 were designed around a much older model of potential bias. They focus primarily on investigators’ financial relationships with the sponsor, including outcome-dependent payments, equity interests, product ownership and significant payments of other sorts. A personal financial position on the outcome of a trial does not fit neatly into that framework.

Nor is the risk limited to investigators (although, to be honest, in never was). 
Before top-line results are publicly released, sponsor personnel, CRO statisticians and programmers, data-management teams, committee members, specialist vendors and subcontractors may already know whether the primary endpoint was met. 

A binary event contract potentially allows that knowledge to be monetized more directly than trading shares in the sponsor.

The concern is not only deliberate misconduct. Financial interests can create actual or perceived challenges to impartiality when decisions remain concerning data anomalies, protocol deviations, adjudication, sensitivity analyses, interpretation or the wording and timing of a top-line announcement.

Sponsors may therefore need to look beyond existing FDA disclosure forms. 

For exampl, can they demonstrate that everyone with access to unblinded or top-line data was identified, prohibited from trading, appropriately trained and covered by contractual obligations? Does that extend through every CRO, vendor and subcontractor?

Prediction markets may provide a useful public forecasting signal (for what ot's worth), but they also demonstrate how quickly financial innovation can outpace a regulatory framework written for another era.

Sportsbooks, Prediction Markets Celebrate Their Own World Cup Trophies 

Spain won the World Cup. The real story is who won the market.

$27 billion. That's what Kalshi processed in worldwide World Cup trading volume. Its "World Cup Champion" market — $1.893B — is now the single most-traded market in the platform's history.

On Sunday, Kalshi out-downloaded @Polymarket, @Coinbase, DraftKings Inc., and FanDuel. Combined. (267K to 190K)

That's not a rounding error. That's a shift.

Here's the part most people will skip past: Polymarket did $4.327B on final day alone world wide. Argentina pulled the most money to win it all — $174.3M. Spain, the actual champion, wasn't far behind at $152.2M.

The crowd backed the favorite. The money was closer than the headlines suggest.

Same pattern shows up in the tickets: Argentina drew 61% of tickets in the "Lift the Trophy" market at Caesars Sportsbook & Casino. But only 53% of the handle. Retail loves a favorite. Capital is more skeptical.

Meanwhile the traditional books didn't get lapped — they got their own records.

Hard Rock Digital called it equal to "10 Super Bowls."  

FanDuel's Final: 200x their previous soccer handle. 

Caesars: biggest soccer event in company history, up 65%.

Sportradar: 350M tickets across 250 books, +129% over 2022 World Cup — on only 62% more matches.

GeoComply:  There were 14.7 million geolocation checks during Sunday's final from U.S. sports bettors 

Everyone won volume. Not everyone won price.

A 104-match pricing study found Polymarket had the best line in every single game. Kalshi beat every sportsbook in 57 of the rest. DraftKings, 38. FanDuel, 7. 

The gap is structural, not seasonal — tighter spreads, deeper liquidity, better data feeds.

The real test isn't this World Cup. It's the 2026 NFL season. The Seattle Seahawks begin their Super Bowl defense 7 weeks from Wednesday. 

That's where we find out if prediction markets' pricing edge is permanent or just a hot streak.

Attackers don't need to breach you. They just need to register something that looks like you.

We checked 24 well-known companies across DeFi, prediction markets, and fintech for lookalike domains. Not "does this look risky" guesswork. Live DNS resolution against typosquat permutations, then registration age and TLS certificate timing cross-checked for every hit.

341 registered, resolving lookalike domains found. Median of 13.5 per brand.

Only 1 is currently flagged by public blocklists as active phishing or malware.

Read that as a floor, not a ceiling. Blocklists catch a domain once it's already serving malicious content. The other 340 sit unclassified. Some are the brands' own defensive registrations, others are dormant infrastructure waiting to be activated. A domain doesn't need to fail a safety check to be dangerous. It just needs to exist when someone decides to use it.

Fintech carried ~50% more lookalike surface per brand than DeFi or prediction markets and a lot of it follows textbook credential-phishing naming: login-, -verify, -support, my- stacked onto the real brand name. That's not random typo noise. That's intent.

What Happens if CLARITY Fails? 

Prediction markets just cut the odds of Congress passing crypto's most important piece of legislation in half, from roughly 70% in May to 31% today. 

The CLARITY Act would finally resolve a regulatory dispute that's divided the industry since 2017: what counts as a security versus a commodity, and who gets to regulate it. The Senate has until the August 7 recess to act. Miss that window, and this bill likely doesn't move again until a September calendar dominated by government funding deadlines and midterm politics. 

Two fights are driving the decline in odds. First, an unresolved ethics dispute increasingly complicated by the president's disclosed crypto income, which several Democratic senators say must be addressed before they'll support the bill. Second, a quieter but consequential bank lobby campaign against Section 404, the provision governing stablecoin rewards, which banks argue functions too much like deposit interest and threatens to pull funding out of the banking system. 

If the bill fails, the industry isn't left with nothing: a March 2026 SEC-CFTC joint interpretation currently classifies bitcoin and other digital assets as commodities. But a 2024 Supreme Court ruling means courts no longer have to defer to that kind of agency interpretation, so it offers policy, not durable legal certainty. 

Bitcoin's stake in all this is smaller than most of the industry's. It's already been treated as a commodity since 2015, with regulated futures and spot ETFs in place. What CLARITY would actually unlock for bitcoin is deeper integration into bank custody, lending, and margining infrastructure, a meaningful structural upgrade, even if it's not a change in bitcoin's basic legal classification. 

We break down the full legislative math, what's really at stake in the Section 404 fight, and what passage or failure means for bitcoin versus the rest of the industry in this week's research.

Let's break it down.

1. Speed and Volume: AI-driven trading systems operate at lightning speed, handling vast volumes of data. This capability can lead to significant market shifts in seconds. If multiple AI systems react simultaneously to a market anomaly, it could trigger a cascade of rapid trades, destabilizing markets.

2. Complex Algorithms: AI systems use complex algorithms to predict market trends. However, they are only as good as the data they are fed. Poor data quality or unforeseen events can lead to inaccurate predictions and misguided trades.

3. Lack of Human Judgment: AI lacks human intuition and emotional intelligence. While it excels at crunching numbers, it doesn't understand the nuances of market sentiment. This can lead to decisions that seem logical on paper but are catastrophic in practice.

4. Interconnected Systems: Financial markets are globally interconnected. A misstep in one market, exacerbated by AI, can ripple across borders. This interconnectedness means that a failure in one AI system can potentially lead to wider market disruptions.

5. Regulatory Challenges: As AI becomes more ingrained in financial markets, regulatory bodies struggle to keep pace. Ensuring that AI systems act within safe and ethical boundaries is crucial, but regulations are still evolving.

Despite these risks, it's important to note that AI also brings significant benefits to the financial markets, such as increased efficiency, improved accuracy, and the ability to process vast amounts of data quickly. The key is in implementing robust oversight and ensuring that AI systems are designed with fail-safes and ethical guidelines.

1️⃣ Data Audit: Identify if your current database is plagued with duplicates, missing values, or obsolete information. 2️⃣ Standardize Inputs: Build strict operational processes to ensure every piece of information collected across all touchpoints is accurate from the start. 3️⃣ Foster a Data Culture: Ensure your team understands that precise data entry is a vital foundation for growth, not just a tedious administrative chore.
AI is like a Formula 1 race car, but clean data is the high-octane fuel. You can't pour water into the tank and expect to cross the finish line first.

A profitable strategy is only half the equation.
If your system can't consume, process, and react to market data in real time, the edge exists only on paper.

Building this project changed the way I think about quantitative engineering.

The future is inference, agents, and databases.

9h • 

Connect

Today Hard Rock Digital runs a single logical database across 8 states and 100 nodes at peak load — handling millions of concurrent users across regulated markets, with Wire Act compliance handled at the database layer, not the application layer.

Joe Rizzo, Senior Platform Architect at Hard Rock Digital, described what that shift felt like at AWS re:Invent 2024: "Instead of many databases spread across the United States, we have one logical database, one logical system, one thing that we're managing and running regardless of the jurisdiction. Instead of investing all the capital to manage and monitor and run these disparate database installations, we can just collapse everything into a single CockroachDB cluster — and distribute the nodes to the geographic locations where they're needed." And: "With CockroachDB, because we don't have to duplicate services, we have faster time to market with lower costs — and we give our players a better experience."

FanDuel took a different path in — they migrated their mission-critical financial ledger off MySQL Galera (Percona) to CockroachDB. The migration was, in their words, "actually super simple." The results: one shared wallet across 7 product lines, 28 state deployments spanning AWS Regions, Outposts, and Local Zones, 4× the scale of their previous Percona baseline at ~5,000 TPS with zero performance issues, and RPO 0 in the event of an outage.

## What makes universal wallet a strategic growth initiative instead of a strategic constraint?

The operators who win the next phase of betting and gaming aren't just building better products. They're building on a foundation that makes regulatory complexity invisible — to players and to engineers alike.

When a player moves from a sportsbook bet to a prediction market to a fantasy lineup, they shouldn't be thinking about which account their money is in. They should just play. When a compliance team needs an audit trail for New York regulators, they shouldn't trigger a query across 50 isolated clusters. They should run one query against one database.

That's the difference between a universal wallet built on the right foundation and one duct-taped together from isolated parts. One is a product. The other is a permanent operational tax on every feature you try to ship.

Universal wallet is a 10-year architectural decision being made right now — at a moment of inflection for the entire industry. The operators who get this right will run faster product teams, cleaner compliance operations, and more resilient businesses. They'll treat regulatory complexity as an engineering problem that's already been solved — not as a permanent drag on every product they try to build.

Hard Rock Digital solved it. FanDuel solved it. The architecture is proven, in production, in regulated U.S. states, right now.

The question is whether you want to be the operator who builds on that foundation from the start — or the one who rebuilds from the wrong one at twice the scale.

The operators who pushed back hardest on this at SBC were, almost without exception, the ones who had already built their sportsbooks on per-state PostgreSQL or MySQL — and were now facing the same decision again, at twice the scale, for five product lines instead of one.

## What is the true TCO of managing isolated database clusters per jurisdiction across 50 states?

Most operators calculate database cost in infrastructure spend — servers, storage, cloud credits. That number is visible and budgetable. It's also not the real story.

The real cost shows up in headcount.

A 25-state isolated-cluster architecture requires a dedicated DBA team to manage 25 independent database instances — patching, tuning, failover testing; a middleware and reconciliation engineering team to maintain the ETL pipelines synchronizing cross-state balances, loyalty data, and rewards currency across every database; and compliance engineering overhead for every regulatory change that requires 25 coordinated deployments and 25 separate audit trail updates. These are engineers whose primary job is maintaining database infrastructure — not building features, not shipping new product lines, not launching new markets.

At $200,000 fully-loaded per engineer, every headcount consumed by infrastructure maintenance is engineering capacity not deployed against your product roadmap. At scale, that's the difference between shipping prediction market integrations this quarter and shipping them late.

Mick Giles, SVP and Head of Technology at Hard Rock Digital, put it plainly: "When it comes to TCO, we are saving a tremendous amount of money and manpower by using CockroachDB. It takes care of all the heavy lifting for our team, and allows us to focus on aggressively entering new markets and reaching our goals."

The honest trade-off: a distributed database foundation costs more in software license. That's real and shouldn't be papered over. The question is whether the labor you eliminate, the downtime you prevent, and the engineering capacity you reclaim justify the investment. CockroachDB customers have found their overall database ownership costs reduced by nearly 70% — the majority of those savings coming from labor, not infrastructure.

## What does the right architecture actually look like — and who's already running it?

Two operators in regulated U.S. sports betting have already solved this problem. Both are worth understanding.

Hard Rock Digital started where most operators still are today — one database per state, AWS Outpost deployments, Postgres-based stack. The team considered maintaining separate databases in each state indefinitely, but the TCO of doing that across all 50 states was, in their words, "astronomical." They chose CockroachDB. Brands that never paid FIFA.

Buying media and designing for attention are two completely different strategies.

A larger budget can buy reach through ad placements.

The companies winning today aren't simply spending more on distribution.
They're engineering products, campaigns, and experiences people want to follow until the very end.

Whether FIFA's decision was good for football is still debatable.
But for marketers, it reinforces something we've known for years.
The biggest competitive advantage isn't buying attention. It's designing experiences that earn it.

The future is inference, agents, and databases.

10h • 

Connect

A complete cross-product betting wallet must support the following features:

1. **Deposits & Withdrawals** — Real-time settlement via bank transfer, Apple Pay, PayPal, and Venmo.
2. **Wagering Balance** — Live cash balance with low-latency reads and writes for peak event traffic.
3. **Wallet Unification** — One balance spanning sportsbook and casino/iGaming.
4. **Rewards Earning** — Automatic, risk-based accrual posted in real time.
5. **Redemption** — Flexible conversion to bonus bets or casino credits.
6. **Cross-Ecosystem Spend** — Usable across gear, collectibles, and live events.
7. **Tiered Loyalty** — Unified tier-point accrual alongside real-money balances.
8. **Wire Act Residency** — Geo-partitioned data storage within the originating state.
9. **Account Segregation** — Legally distinct balances per state, transparent to the player.
10. **Audit & Reporting** — Immutable, real-time ledgers for gaming regulators.
11. **Prediction Markets** — Unified login and wallet for emerging wagering products.
12. **Retail Sportsbook** — Physical cashier sync with digital wallet balances.
13. **Cross-State Promotions** — Promotional flows routed through regional hub clusters.

Items 8 through 10 are where most wallet builds encounter challenges. These are not just product features; they are regulatory requirements embedded in the architecture. This is why the universal wallet is fundamentally a database design problem.

Why can't a single database support a wallet spanning sportsbook, fantasy, and prediction markets? The Wire Act imposes restrictions that prevent this, as US gaming regulations mandate that bets must be placed and settled within the same jurisdiction. The transaction record must physically reside in the state where the bet originated, which is a constraint that cannot be addressed at the application layer but must be integrated into the database architecture.

Most operators have opted for the straightforward solution: one database per state. For example, a PostgreSQL instance or a MySQL cluster located in New Jersey for New Jersey bets, and similar setups for other states.

PostgreSQL users typically utilize RDS on AWS Outpost, which is familiar and cost-effective. MySQL users often implement MySQL Galera clusters or community distributions like Percona XtraDB Cluster, managing each state separately with custom middleware.

Both strategies face a critical issue: they are manageable only at a small scale. With 25 states across five product lines — sportsbook, prediction markets, fantasy, eCommerce, and collectibles — this results in 125 integration points, each needing its own administration, monitoring, reconciliation pipeline, and compliance workflow. At 50 states, this number escalates to 250. 

Continued in Part 3. Follow along — it gets interesting. 🃏

Thinking about clinical trial enrollment

10h • 


Follow

Lots of hot takes on Kalshi's announcement that they're entering the clinical trials prediction market!

Most takes seem to have two major points: (a) this is icky, and (b) this poses serious problems for the industry (mostly around perverse incentives and/or insider trading).

I certainly endorse point (a). It all feels pretty gross. 

But I'm skeptical, at least for now, on point (b). A couple points:

 - This has actually been going on for a while. There are Kalshi markets on FDA approvals from over a year ago. The only thing that seems to be actually new right now is that they've onboarded a partner to help set rules and adjudicate results.

 - The numbers are incredibly small. You can get a 6x payout if you bet on Lilly's retratrutide getting approved this year (which seems incredibly doubtful in a post-Makary world, but sure, maybe!). The problem is that there's only about $18,000 TOTAL active in that market. And this doesn't run like a sportsbook - your bet only happens if someone else is willing to take the other side.

 - The new rules also state that prediction for individual trials will only open **after** enrollment is complete in those trials, meaning they have no impact on enrollment. 

I am not defending Kalshi here (see point a, above). But I do think it is more silly than dangerous. Folks who have actual, useful information about how trials and drugs may succeed already have clear opportunities to monetize that information much more effectively than this.

I am still slogging through their new white paper though, so perhaps there are interesting threads worth pulling here.
